data.title: Assessing Nestlé Stock Value After Expansion into Affordable Nutrition Products
data.description:
Deciding what to do with Nestlé stock these days is far from straightforward. If you’ve held shares, you know the ride hasn't been smooth. Just look at the numbers: while the stock has bounced back 7.5% over the last week and surged 14.2% over the past month, it’s still down about 0.7% over the last year, and even further over three and five years. The question is, does this momentum signal a long-awaited turnaround or is there more volatility ahead?

Recent headlines have added fuel to the conversation. For instance, there has been growing attention on Nestlé’s expansion into affordable nutrition products, especially in emerging markets, as well as strategic investments in plant-based foods. These moves have re-energized some investor optimism around growth, potentially explaining the uptick in short-term gains. However, the market’s mood hasn’t fully shifted and questions about long-term profitability and global demand still hang in the air.

Now, with so much changing, how is the company stacking up on value? Using six different checks to gauge whether a stock is undervalued, Nestlé scores 4 out of 6. That’s a solid showing, but it isn’t the full story. Let’s walk through how those valuation approaches break down. After looking at the numbers, stick around for a smarter way to think about valuation that could help you make even better stock decisions.

Why Nestlé is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Nestlé Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model offers a lens into Nestlé’s true worth by projecting the company's future free cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. This approach aims to strip away market noise and focus on the core earning power of the business over time.

Currently, Nestlé generates CHF 8.8 Billion in free cash flow. Analysts estimate steady growth over the next five years, and projections see free cash flow rising to CHF 11.9 Billion by 2029. For the years beyond analyst coverage, expert models extrapolate continued, though moderating, growth. All figures are based on cash flows reported in Swiss Francs (CHF).

Based on these projections, the DCF model calculates an intrinsic value of CHF 144.01 per share. Given that this value represents a 43.2% discount compared to the current share price, Nestlé shares appear significantly undervalued according to this analysis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Nestlé.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Nestlé is undervalued by 43.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Nestlé Price vs Earnings

The price-to-earnings (PE) ratio is a classic yardstick for valuing profitable companies like Nestlé because it relates a company’s share price directly to its earnings, giving investors a snapshot of what the market is willing to pay for each franc of profit. This makes it especially relevant for mature businesses with steady earnings streams.

However, what counts as a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio is not set in stone. It is influenced by growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk often justify a higher PE, while slower growth or higher uncertainty typically pull it down.

Currently, Nestlé trades at a PE ratio of 20.43x. For comparison, the average PE ratio for the broader Food industry is 16.15x and the average for Nestlé’s peers comes in much higher at 31.03x. This puts Nestlé somewhere in between, ahead of the average food stock but far below the peer group’s lofty valuations.

To refine this picture, we turn to Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio.” This proprietary metric estimates what PE multiple Nestlé actually deserves based on factors like its earnings growth, profit margin, market cap, industry, and risk profile. This delivers a customized benchmark rather than a one-size-fits-all average. This individualized approach provides a clearer sense of value than a straightforward industry or peer comparison ever could.

According to the Fair Ratio, a justifiable multiple for Nestlé today would be 28.80x. Compared to its current 20.43x, Nestlé’s shares look attractively valued on this measure, suggesting the market may not be fully recognizing its strengths or future prospects.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Nestlé Narrative

Earlier we mentioned there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is simply the story you believe about Nestlé’s future. It connects your perspective on the company’s business (why it can succeed or struggle) to your expectations for future revenue, earnings, and profit margins, and, ultimately, to what you believe the stock is really worth.

With Narratives, you do not just look at numbers in isolation. You explain your reasoning, make your own forecasts, and arrive at your own Fair Value. You can access and build Narratives directly on Simply Wall St’s Community page, where millions of investors do the same. This makes the tool both accessible and easy to use.

Narratives help you decide whether Nestlé is worth buying or selling by letting you compare your Fair Value (based on your story) with today’s price. They update automatically when new information or news arrives, so your analysis stays relevant.

For example, one investor may write a bullish Narrative using optimistic forecasts and assign a Fair Value up at CHF 103.0, expecting growth from digital transformation and China expansion. A more cautious Narrative might target CHF 71.0, citing margin pressure and slower demand as key risks.

Whenever a stock posts some pretty sharp ups and downs, it gets everyone’s attention, and Fluor has definitely kept things interesting. After a bumpy week with the price dropping 8.4%, it’s easy to wonder if there’s more turbulence ahead or if these dips are opportunities in disguise. This mixed performance isn’t happening in a vacuum. Fluor has made headlines recently as it’s expanded into new project markets and emphasized sustainability in its operations. These trends could shift how investors look at the company’s value. Approach 1: Fluor Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s intrinsic value by projecting its future free cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value to account for the time value of money. This approach essentially tries to answer how much today’s investor should pay for all the future cash Fluor is expected to generate.

The P/E ratio lets investors see how much they're paying for each dollar of earnings. A lower P/E can mean the market expects little growth or sees higher risk in the company, while a higher P/E often reflects strong future growth expectations or lower perceived risk.

data.title: Should You Reconsider CrowdStrike After Shares Surged 67% and More Growth Is Priced In?
data.description:
So you are thinking about what to do with CrowdStrike Holdings, and honestly, who can blame you? Just when you think this cybersecurity giant might take a breather, it surprises investors again. Over the past year, the stock has rocketed an eye-catching 67.1%, and if you zoom out a bit more, returns have stacked up to 209.1% over three years and a remarkable 280.1% over five. Even after such a stellar run, recent weeks have seen more positive momentum, with a 2.3% lift over the last seven days and 1.4% over the past month. Year to date, shares are already up a robust 44.0%.

What is fueling all this optimism? Part of it is the market’s ongoing recognition of CrowdStrike’s leadership in endpoint security and cloud protection. Many enterprises, and even government agencies, continue to double down on cyber defense. This trend plays right into CrowdStrike’s hands. News of big customer wins and the company’s expansion into new international markets have reinforced the story of long-term growth potential, even as risk perceptions shift in response to threats and innovation across the sector. These factors have added life to the price, but they also put a spotlight on how the market is valuing the company.

That brings us to the key question: does the current price tag make sense? Using six standard valuation criteria, CrowdStrike scores a 0 right now. In other words, by our usual checks, none signal the stock as undervalued. Of course, numbers never tell the full story, and some valuation approaches are more telling than others. Let’s dig into the methods most investors use to weigh value, and later, I will share what I think is the most insightful perspective of all.

CrowdStrike Holdings scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: CrowdStrike Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates CrowdStrike Holdings' intrinsic value by projecting future cash flows from the business and discounting them to their present value using a required rate of return. This approach helps investors gauge what the company's underlying cash generation is worth today, separate from market hype.

Currently, CrowdStrike has a Free Cash Flow (FCF) of $1.04 billion. Analyst estimates drive the first five years of growth, with projections reaching up to $4.58 billion in FCF by 2030. Beyond initial analyst forecasts, further FCF growth estimates are extended by Simply Wall St using a logical extrapolation model. These projections reflect the company’s anticipated rapid expansion in the cybersecurity market as well as improvements in scaling efficiency as it grows larger.

Using this model, the fair value of CrowdStrike shares is calculated at $414.88. However, with the current market price, this means the stock is around 20.5% above its estimated intrinsic value based on cash flow fundamentals. Investors are factoring in aggressive growth and future profitability, but at present, the DCF suggests the shares are trading at a premium.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: CrowdStrike Holdings Price vs Sales

For companies like CrowdStrike Holdings that are scaling rapidly but may not yet be consistently profitable, the Price-to-Sales (P/S) ratio is a highly useful valuation metric. It provides a window into how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of the company’s sales, which can be more meaningful for high-growth tech companies than earnings-based multiples.

The "right" P/S ratio often depends on growth prospects and industry risk. High-growth companies or those with a significant competitive edge usually command higher P/S multiples. Riskier or slower-growth businesses tend to trade at lower valuations. It is all about how optimistic investors are about future expansion and profitability.

CrowdStrike's current P/S ratio stands at a lofty 28.9x. This sharply exceeds both the broader software industry average of 5.2x and the peer group average of 15.0x. However, Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for CrowdStrike, which synthesizes unique factors like growth, margins, risk, sector, and scale, recommends a P/S multiple of 17.6x. This metric gives a more tailored valuation snapshot than simple industry or peer comparisons because it accounts for what truly makes CrowdStrike different, such as its rapid sales momentum, market opportunities, and risk profile.

At 28.9x, CrowdStrike’s actual P/S remains notably above the Fair Ratio of 17.6x. This suggests the stock’s valuation is stretched based on current fundamentals and growth expectations.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Lockheed Martin (LMT) Margin Decline Challenges Bullish Narratives Despite Strong Valuation and Earnings Quality
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) posted a net profit margin of 5.7%, down from 9.4% last year, with average annual earnings slipping 4.6% over the past five years. While the recent trend shows slower growth, forecasts call for annual profit growth of 14.3% and revenue growth of 3.2% per year, both lagging the broader US market. Despite this, the company’s earnings quality remains high, and the stock stands out for its attractive valuation, solid dividend, and continued growth expectations, helping to keep investor sentiment steady.

See our full analysis for Lockheed Martin.

Now, let's see how the latest results measure up against the most widely held narratives for Lockheed Martin. Where do the numbers back up the market story, and where do they challenge it?

See what the community is saying about Lockheed Martin

Advertisement

Profit Margin Slide Reflects Legacy Program Strain

  • Lockheed Martin’s net profit margin dropped to 5.7%, a clear contraction from 9.4% a year ago, largely due to persistent cost overruns and integration challenges in major legacy programs.
  • Analysts' consensus view points to how margin pressures are set against a backdrop of ongoing operational improvements and enhanced risk controls.
    • Contract discipline and digital transformation initiatives are expected to support margin recovery, but legacy program charges, such as the $1.8 billion hit expected in Q2 2025, threaten near-term profitability.
    • This struggle aligns with consensus concerns that further profit compression could occur if technical hurdles and budget risks persist across Lockheed’s core classified and helicopter programs.
  • Analysts see Lockheed’s push for efficiency as potentially turning the tide, but only if cost discipline sticks.
    • Rising U.S. munitions spending and multi-year contracts could stabilize margins, but overexposure to large legacy projects still creates headwinds in the short term.
    • Revenue growth visibility remains robust, yet improving profits depends critically on avoiding further cost overruns, especially in fixed-price contracts.

Valuation Advantage Versus Peers Holds Up

  • The stock trades at a lower price-to-earnings ratio than its industry and peer average, while also sitting significantly below DCF fair value ($487.14 per share compared to a DCF fair value of $575.19), indicating analysts see Lockheed Martin as attractively priced given expected earnings growth.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative argues that Lockheed Martin’s fair pricing rests on forecasts for rising profit margins, from 5.9% today to 8.7% in three years, and revenue expected to reach $81 billion by 2028.
    • The consensus price target of $520.41 sits just above the current share price, suggesting the market mostly agrees with these optimistic forecasts, but there is debate: some analysts project much higher or lower targets depending on how cost and contract risks play out.
    • Consensus weighs Lockheed’s attractive PE discount and fair pricing against a backdrop of budget uncertainty and heightened competition, leaving the door open for re-rating if margins do improve.
  • On valuation metrics, Lockheed’s PE ratio is not only below the US Aerospace & Defense average (currently 34.4x) but also expected to compress further to 18.9x by 2028 if projections materialize.
    • A relatively small 4.1% upside to the consensus price target implies much of the good news may already be priced in unless earnings or margins outpace current analyst expectations.
    • The gap between today’s price and DCF fair value offers a cushion, especially if Lockheed can deliver on backlog and technology-driven margin expansion.

Backlog and R&D Investment Underpin Long-Term Case

  • Analyst consensus highlights robust demand for advanced platforms like the F-35, PAC-3, and hypersonic weapons, fueling sustained backlog and providing strong visibility for revenue and cash flow stability across multiple years.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative notes that long-term revenue and earnings durability rely on continued investment in next-generation technologies, such as hypersonics and missile defense.
    • Strong public-private R&D partnerships and a diversified product pipeline support the view that Lockheed can expand into higher-margin, future-ready markets.
    • However, pressure from intensifying competition and shifting defense budgets underscore the need for consistent innovation to avoid revenue stagnation or share losses.
